prompt: Recommend three works of fiction that you think every thoughtful adult should read at some point. Explain each in two or three sentences.
response:
1. 1984 – George Orwell  
A short, cold punch to the solar plexus that shows how language, memory and fear can be engineered until love itself looks like treason. Once you see how the last sentence of the novel inverts the first, you’ll never trust a political slogan—or your own passivity—again.

2. Beloved – Toni Morrison  
Morrison turns the statistical abstraction “sixty million and more” into one haunted house and one murdered child, forcing you to feel the ongoing cost of slavery on a mother’s heartbeat and a nation’s conscience. The prose itself is incantatory, refusing to let historical horror settle into safely archived past.

3. The Remains of the Day – Kazuo Ishiguro  
A butler’s polite, comic, ultimately shattering inventory of the days he traded love, moral courage and authenticity for the illusion of “dignity” in service to power. It is the quietest cautionary tale ever written about how professionalism can become an alibi for a wasted life.
